title: OT Network Segmentation and Zone-Conduit Enforcement (Purdue Model)
exclusions: Excludes traffic on a single Purdue level (intra-level flows are not subject to the cross-zone rule and would be too noisy here). Excludes ICMP echo / NTP heartbeat / DNS flows on conduits that are explicitly allow-listed in `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv`. Excludes flows during documented commissioning windows when temporary cross-zone connections are required (controlled by UC-22.51.9's change-control mechanism).
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.51.5` archived every 15 minutes to `audit_evidence` with `otcc_clause="OTCC-2-5-3-1"`. Each row preserves the (src_ip, src_zone, dest_ip, dest_zone, src_purdue_level, dest_purdue_level, app_protocol, industrial_protocol_function_code, bytes_in, bytes_out, conduit_id_expected, conduit_id_observed, violation_type, first_seen, last_seen). Dashboard `NCA OTCC – Zone-Conduit Enforcement` renders the live Purdue diagram with current violations highlighted; a 30-day trend per conduit is exported quarterly for the OT cybersecurity steering committee.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Continuously evidences zone-conduit enforcement: every cross-zone flow is captured, classified against the documented zone-conduit allowlist, and any violation surfaces in the SOC within 15 minutes. Combined with the documented Purdue zone diagram (an OT cybersecurity engineering artefact, not produced by this UC), this is sufficient evidence of OTCC-2-5-3-1 — hence 'full'.
compliance.controlObjective: Proves OT network segmentation is enforced by detecting any traffic that crosses a zone boundary outside an approved conduit, any non-approved protocol on a conduit, or any flow that violates the Purdue layered model.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.51.5` archived to `audit_evidence` plus the `NCA OTCC – Zone-Conduit Enforcement` dashboard. The quarterly PDF export of the per-conduit trend chart, signed by Head of OT Security, is the formal artefact filed for NCA inspection.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Configure a test workstation in the corporate (Purdue Level 4) network to send a Modbus/TCP request (port 502) to an in-scope PLC in the OT control (Purdue Level 1) network, deliberately bypassing the OT DMZ jump-host. Within 15 minutes UC-22.51.5 MUST flag the flow with `violation_type="purdue_layer_jump"` and `industrial_protocol="modbus"`, route the alert to the SOC and to the OT network engineering on-call, and the dashboard MUST surface the offending source IP and PLC.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Configure the same workstation to send a Modbus/TCP request via the documented historian-replication conduit (Level 4 → OT DMZ → Level 3 historian), where the conduit is in `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv` with the allowed source zone, destination zone, source IP range, destination IP range, and protocol set. UC-22.51.5 MUST NOT flag the flow.

dataSources: `index=ot_cybervision sourcetype="cisco:cybervision:flow"` for industrial-protocol-aware flow records, OT-firewall and DMZ-firewall logs (`index=network sourcetype="pan:traffic"`, `cisco:asa`, `fortinet:traffic`, `checkpoint:firewall`), optional `index=network sourcetype="stream:tcp"` from Splunk Stream wire data. Lookups: `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv` (approved conduits), `ot_zone_inventory.csv` (CIDR/subnet → zone → Purdue level), `ot_change_window.csv` (commissioning grace).
app: Splunk Add-on for Cisco Cyber Vision (Splunkbase 6071), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757), Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), optional Splunk_TA_stream (Splunkbase 1809).

splunkbaseApps.name: Splunk Add-on for Cisco Cyber Vision
Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

spl:
(index=ot_cybervision sourcetype="cisco:cybervision:flow" earliest=-15m@m) OR (index=network (sourcetype="pan:traffic" OR sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="fortinet:traffic") action="allow" earliest=-15m@m)
| eval src_ip=coalesce(src_ip, src), dest_ip=coalesce(dest_ip, dst, dest)
| lookup ot_zone_inventory.csv ip AS src_ip OUTPUT zone AS src_zone purdue_level AS src_purdue_level
| lookup ot_zone_inventory.csv ip AS dest_ip OUTPUT zone AS dest_zone purdue_level AS dest_purdue_level
| where isnotnull(src_zone) AND isnotnull(dest_zone) AND src_zone!=dest_zone
| eval app_protocol=coalesce(app, app_protocol, application)
| eval industrial_protocol=coalesce(industrial_protocol_name, application_subtype)
| lookup ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT conduit_id allowed_src_cidr allowed_dest_cidr allowed_protocols
| eval src_in_allowed_range=if(isnotnull(allowed_src_cidr) AND cidrmatch(allowed_src_cidr, src_ip), "true", "false")
| eval dest_in_allowed_range=if(isnotnull(allowed_dest_cidr) AND cidrmatch(allowed_dest_cidr, dest_ip), "true", "false")
| eval protocol_allowed=if(isnotnull(allowed_protocols) AND match(allowed_protocols, "(^|,)".coalesce(industrial_protocol, app_protocol)."(,|$)"), "true", "false")
| eval purdue_layer_jump=if(abs(coalesce(tonumber(dest_purdue_level), 0) - coalesce(tonumber(src_purdue_level), 0)) > 1, "true", "false")
| eval violation_type = case(
    isnull(conduit_id), "unauthorised_conduit",
    src_in_allowed_range="false" OR dest_in_allowed_range="false", "out_of_range_endpoint",
    protocol_allowed="false", "unauthorised_protocol",
    purdue_layer_jump="true", "purdue_layer_jump",
    1=1, null())
| where isnotnull(violation_type)
| stats earliest(_time) AS first_seen latest(_time) AS last_seen sum(bytes_in) AS bytes_in sum(bytes_out) AS bytes_out values(industrial_protocol_function_code) AS function_codes count BY src_ip src_zone src_purdue_level dest_ip dest_zone dest_purdue_level app_protocol industrial_protocol violation_type conduit_id
| sort - count
description: Detects any cross-zone OT traffic that is not part of a documented approved conduit, any flow whose source or destination falls outside the approved IP range for a conduit, any conduit hosting an unauthorised protocol, and any flow that skips a Purdue layer (Level 4 → Level 1 directly, for example).
value: Network segmentation is the single most testable OT cybersecurity control and the one NCA inspectors test most aggressively: they will scan an in-scope OT IP from a corporate IP and expect to be blocked. This UC raises an alarm in the SOC the moment any unblocked path becomes active, and produces the per-conduit evidence record that demonstrates ongoing enforcement — not just a point-in-time architecture diagram.
implementation: (1) Catalogue every OT zone (Process, Control, DCS, ICS-DMZ, Corporate) and assign Purdue levels 0–5 in `ot_zone_inventory.csv` with CIDR mappings; refresh from the network-design source of truth daily. (2) Document every approved conduit in `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv` (src_zone, dest_zone, conduit_id, allowed_src_cidr, allowed_dest_cidr, allowed_protocols). (3) Onboard OT firewall and DMZ firewall logs into `index=network`. (4) Deploy Cisco Cyber Vision sensors and onboard flow records into `index=ot_cybervision`. (5) Schedule UC-22.51.5 every 15 minutes; route violations to ES notable with severity=critical.
visualization: (Panel 1) Purdue-model layered diagram with current violations highlighted (Sankey or custom flow viz). (Panel 2) Sortable table: every distinct (src_zone, dest_zone, violation_type) triple seen in the last hour. (Panel 3) 30-day timechart of `count by conduit_id` for approved conduits — useful for capacity-planning. (Panel 4) 30-day timechart of `count by violation_type` for unapproved flows — trend tells the steering committee whether segmentation is improving or eroding.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Cisco Cyber Vision
ISA/IEC 62443-3-2: Security Risk Assessment for System Design (Zones and Conduits)
Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture — layered model overview
Splunk CIM: Network Traffic data model

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Backup / replication windows** — large historian-to-corporate replication can exceed allowed-protocol or allowed-byte thresholds if the conduit allowlist is missing the SCP/SFTP protocol. Suppression: add the data-replication conduit to the allowlist explicitly; do not blanket-allow via `*`.

2. **Sensor restart blind spots** — Cisco Cyber Vision sensor restarts cause flows to be recorded against the next available sensor; the first 5 minutes after restart may produce ghost flows. Suppression: filter `_time` to exclude sensor-restart windows from `ot_cybervision_sensors.csv` events.

3. **NAT / proxy on the DMZ** — if the DMZ jump host NATs requests outbound, the OT-side log shows the jump host's IP rather than the original source. The conduit allowlist must specifically allow the jump host as the source. Suppression: review the conduit allowlist whenever DMZ NAT topology changes.

4. **VRRP / HSRP heartbeat** — router-redundancy heartbeats span zones in some legacy designs. Suppression: explicit allowlist row with protocol=VRRP/HSRP, src and dest=router-redundancy-VLAN.

5. **Multicast OT protocols** — PROFINET DCP, EtherNet/IP Implicit messaging, IEC 61850 GOOSE use L2 multicast and may not be visible at the firewall but ARE visible to Cyber Vision. The conduit allowlist must distinguish 'this is L2 traffic, not L3' so cross-zone multicast at L2 (e.g. across stretched VLANs) is correctly handled. Suppression: explicit handling in the allowlist with `protocol_layer=L2` and `flow_type=multicast`.

**Suppression mechanism**: KV Store `otcc_22_51_5_exceptions` with per-flow grants. The runbook for accepting a flag requires a documented IR investigation and a 30-day maximum grant.
equipmentModels: Cisco Cyber Vision 4.x (industrial zone and conduit map)
Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS 11.x (industrial security policy enforcement)
Splunk Add-on for Cisco Cyber Vision (Splunkbase 6071, asset and flow events)
Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757, TRAFFIC and THREAT logs)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- Splunk Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud. The 15-minute schedule means an active OT estate of ~10k flows/min generates ~150k rows per scheduled run; size the search head accordingly.
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco Cyber Vision (Splunkbase 6071) for industrial-protocol-aware flows.
- Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757) or Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620) or Fortinet (Splunkbase 2846) on the OT-DMZ firewall path. CIM-compliant field aliases must be installed on Search Heads.
- `index=ot_cybervision` (90-day retention online).
- `index=network` (30-day retention online; firewall logs are higher volume).
- `index=audit_evidence` (7-year retention).
- Lookups:
  - `ot_zone_inventory.csv` — owned by OT network engineering, refreshed daily. Columns: `ip` (CIDR-expanded), `zone`, `purdue_level`, `business_unit`, `criticality_class`, `last_validated_at`.
  - `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv` — owned by OT cybersecurity architecture. Columns: `conduit_id`, `src_zone`, `dest_zone`, `allowed_src_cidr`, `allowed_dest_cidr`, `allowed_protocols` (comma-separated), `protocol_layer` (L2 / L3 / L4), `flow_type` (unicast / multicast), `change_ticket_id`, `last_reviewed_at`.
  - `ot_cybervision_sensors.csv` for sensor-restart grace windows.
  - KV Store `otcc_22_51_5_exceptions` for manual exceptions.
- OT cybersecurity engineering team's documented Purdue-model diagram and zone-conduit register (in `docs/`); the lookup is mechanical, the diagram is the human reference for auditors.
- ES notable framework configured with `network` security domain.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

The UC reads two streams: (a) Cisco Cyber Vision flow records, which carry industrial-protocol context (Modbus function codes, DNP3 object IDs, EtherNet/IP CIP class/instance), and (b) firewall traffic logs, which carry every L3/L4 flow including non-industrial protocols.

For Cisco Cyber Vision, configure the flow modular input:

```ini
[cisco_cybervision://flows]
endpoint = https://cyber-vision-center.example.com/api
record_type = flow
interval = 60
index = ot_cybervision
sourcetype = cisco:cybervision:flow
```

For the OT-DMZ firewall (Palo Alto example), configure syslog to Heavy Forwarder with sourcetype `pan:traffic`. CIM-compliant field aliases provide `src_ip`, `dest_ip`, `app`, `bytes_in`, `bytes_out`.

Validate field extraction:

```spl
index=ot_cybervision sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:flow earliest=-15m
| fieldsummary
| search field IN (src_ip, dest_ip, industrial_protocol_name, industrial_protocol_function_code, bytes_in, bytes_out)
```

And:

```spl
index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-15m
| fieldsummary
| search field IN (src_ip, dest_ip, app, action, bytes_in, bytes_out)
```

Volume guidance: a typical OT estate generates ~50–500 GB/day of firewall logs and ~10–50 GB/day of Cyber Vision flow records. Plan license accordingly; consider sampling Cyber Vision flow records below severity threshold to reduce volume while preserving the alert-relevant subset.

## Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The search has four sequential pass-throughs joined by `OR`:

1. **Cross-zone detection**: every flow whose source and destination resolve to different zones via `ot_zone_inventory.csv`. Flows within a single zone pass through invisibly.
2. **Conduit existence check**: cross-zone flows whose (src_zone, dest_zone) pair is NOT in `ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv` are `violation_type="unauthorised_conduit"` immediately.
3. **Endpoint range check**: for flows whose conduit exists, verify the source IP falls within `allowed_src_cidr` and destination IP within `allowed_dest_cidr`. Failures are `violation_type="out_of_range_endpoint"`.
4. **Protocol allowance check**: for flows whose conduit and endpoints check out, verify the protocol is in `allowed_protocols`. Failures are `violation_type="unauthorised_protocol"`.
5. **Purdue-jump check**: regardless of conduit, flows whose Purdue levels differ by more than 1 (e.g. Level 4 → Level 1 directly without traversing Level 3 DMZ) are `violation_type="purdue_layer_jump"`. This is the highest-severity violation type because it is the classic 'compromise spreading down the stack' signal.

Schedule:

```ini
[UC-22.51.5: OT Network Segmentation and Zone-Conduit Enforcement]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -15m@m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index, risk, notable
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.51.5,reg=NCA_OTCC,clause=OTCC-2-5-3-1
action.notable.param.rule_title = NCA OTCC zone-conduit violation
action.notable.param.security_domain = network
action.notable.param.severity = critical
alert.severity = 5
```

Notable creation only on `violation_type IN (purdue_layer_jump, unauthorised_conduit)`; `unauthorised_protocol` and `out_of_range_endpoint` route to `audit_evidence` and email only.

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Zone inventory completeness**: every IP in OT firewall logs should resolve to a zone. Run:

```spl
index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic earliest=-1h
| stats count by src_ip dest_ip
| eval ip = mvappend(src_ip, dest_ip)
| mvexpand ip
| lookup ot_zone_inventory.csv ip OUTPUT zone
| stats count by zone
```

A large bucket of `zone=null` means the lookup is incomplete; coordinate with OT network engineering.

**(b) Conduit allowlist sanity**: every allowed conduit must reference a current change ticket:

```spl
| inputlookup ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv
| where isnull(change_ticket_id) OR change_ticket_id=""
```

Non-empty result means the allowlist contains ungoverned conduits — escalate to OT cybersecurity architecture for review.

**(c) Positive control**: from a corporate workstation, attempt a Modbus connection (port 502/tcp) to an in-scope PLC IP that is reachable via your test network. The firewall logs and Cyber Vision flow records should both surface this; the UC must flag it as `purdue_layer_jump`. Capture the alert email and the resulting ES notable as part of the positive-control evidence.

**(d) Negative control**: from the historian relay server (whose conduit is allowlisted), send the same Modbus connection to the same PLC. The UC must NOT flag it. The flow MUST still appear in `audit_evidence` for transparency but with `violation_type=null`.

**(e) Purdue diagram check**: at OT cybersecurity steering committee quarterly, present the Purdue diagram with the dashboard's panel-1 overlay; visual inspection confirms the diagram and the runtime monitoring agree.

## Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard**: `NCA OTCC – Zone-Conduit Enforcement`. Designed for a network engineering audience but readable by auditors.

- **Panel 1, custom visualisation**: layered Purdue diagram (rendered as a Splunk Dashboard Studio custom viz or a Sankey panel from the `splunk-viz-design` skill catalogue). Each link's thickness encodes flow volume; red highlights any link carrying a violation.
- **Panel 2, sortable table**: every distinct (src_zone, dest_zone, violation_type) seen in the last hour with `count`, `bytes`, `industrial_protocol`, and a drill-through to the firewall logs in question.
- **Panel 3, time chart**: 30-day count of violations by type. Trend down is the steering-committee KPI.
- **Panel 4, time chart**: 30-day count of in-conduit flow rate per conduit — useful for capacity planning and for detecting silent conduit failures (a conduit that drops to zero is suspicious).
- **Panel 5, top sources / destinations**: histograms of the top 20 offending src_ip and top 20 offending dest_ip in the last 24 hours.

**Runbook for a `purdue_layer_jump` alert** (owner: SOC L2 + OT network engineering on-call):

1. Within 15 minutes: confirm the alert is genuine (not a sensor or firewall glitch) by checking both the firewall and Cyber Vision sources independently.
2. If genuine: declare a P1 IR. The hypothesis is a corporate-zone host has gained direct L3 reach to an OT control device. Possible root causes: misconfigured firewall rule, recent merger that introduced new routing, lateral movement from a compromised host.
3. Block the offending source IP at the OT-DMZ firewall pending investigation; this is a documented IR action and does not require change-control approval for an active incident.
4. Engage the OT IR runbook (UC-22.51.18). Preserve the firewall logs and Cyber Vision flow records.
5. Post-incident: reconcile the conduit allowlist against the current firewall rule set; if the firewall has a rule the allowlist doesn't, harmonise.

**Runbook for `unauthorised_protocol` and `out_of_range_endpoint`** (owner: OT network engineering, severity P3):

1. Within 2 business days: contact the application owner.
2. Either widen the conduit allowlist via documented change-control (linking the change ticket back to the allowlist row), or block the source/protocol.
3. Document the decision in the OT cybersecurity programme RAID log.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting

**Search returns nothing** — the lookup-build pipeline is empty. Run `| inputlookup ot_zone_inventory.csv | stats count` and `| inputlookup ot_zone_conduit_allowlist.csv | stats count`. Either should be ≥1000 in a real OT estate.

**Every cross-zone flow flags as `unauthorised_conduit`** — the conduit allowlist is keyed on (src_zone, dest_zone) but the zone names in the inventory are slightly different. Use exact-match string comparison and confirm zone names are identical (case, whitespace, accented characters).

**`cidrmatch` fails for IPv6 endpoints** — the `cidrmatch` function in Splunk is IPv4-only as of 9.2. For IPv6, use `match(ip, "^2001:db8:")`-style prefix matching or extend the lookup with explicit IPv6 ranges.

**Search runs for >5 minutes per 15-minute interval** — schedule overlap. Either widen the schedule to every 30 minutes, or restrict the search scope to high-priority zones first and add lower-priority zones on a longer cadence.

**OT-specific failure modes**:

- **L2 multicast invisible at the firewall**: a stretched VLAN crossing the OT DMZ at L2 will not appear in firewall logs at all but will appear in Cyber Vision flows. The allowlist must explicitly note L2 multicast conduits and the runbook for `purdue_layer_jump` must check Cyber Vision specifically when firewall logs are blank.
- **Industrial-protocol gateways**: Modbus-to-OPC-UA gateways are common; the protocol observed at the OT-DMZ firewall is OPC-UA but the upstream protocol toward the PLC is Modbus. The allowlist must support a 'gateway translation' relationship: conduit A permits OPC-UA Level 3 → Level 2, conduit B permits Modbus Level 2 → Level 1, and the gateway is the legitimate endpoint of both.
- **Engineering-workstation roaming**: an EWS at Level 3 may legitimately briefly access Level 4 for documentation lookup. Suppression via documented allowlist entry pinned to the specific EWS host(s), not a blanket EWS-to-Internet conduit.
- **DCS vendor remote-support tunnels**: Schneider, Honeywell, Yokogawa, Emerson, ABB all offer vendor-tunnel remote-support patterns; these are conduits in the OTCC sense and must be in the allowlist with explicit src/dest, allowed protocol, and a per-session UC-22.51.19 mediation.
grandmaExplanation: We watch every connection between the office network and the plant floor. Each allowed connection has rules about who can talk, what they can say, and how. If a computer tries to skip the rules or use the wrong language, we ring the bell.
